ground_truth.title: Backup Strategy
ground_truth.author: Kaufman, Jeff T.
ground_truth.main_content: After reading a post from someone who nearly lost all their data to a joint NAS and external hard drive failure I decided to think through my data durability. Most of my stuff lives in the cloud: email on Gmail, docs in Google Docs, photos in Google Photos, data in Google Drive. I trust Google a lot for this sort of thing, both given their public reputation and as an employee. I think Google is extremely unlikely to lose or corrupt my data. The next biggest place where I have things is on the server that runs jefftk.com. I back this up to my laptop with a cronjob that runs rsync commands to sync my websites, home directory, and /etc to ~/Google Drive. I also have a cronjob set up to run twice a month that backs up the comments on my blog posts. Everything on my laptop is temporary, and anything important is under ~/Google Drive. The main weakness in this setup is its dependency on Drive: accidental deletions could sync up, or losing access to my Google account would be a disaster. I think both of these are too unlikely to be worth mitigating though. They could happen, but they're much less likely than the sort of failure (~1%/year) that you're trying to avoid by backing things up.
with: After reading a post from someone who nearly lost all their data to a joint NAS and external hard drive failure I decided to think through my data durability.
Most of my stuff lives in the cloud: email on Gmail, docs in Google Docs, photos in Google Photos, data in Google Drive.
I back this up to my laptop with a cronjob that looks like: rsync -t -l -r -c ps:jtk/ www-jefftk-com/ --delete-after
Everything on my laptop is temporary, and anything important is under ~/Google Drive.
The main weakness in this setup is its dependency on Drive: for the portion of Google Drive synced to my laptop, if I accidentally deleted or changed things locally that could be synced up.
